What isSQL?
3

Itstands forStructured Query Language. It allows writing database queries to 
retreve, insert, update and delete data( CRUD operations). Also, thecreation 
oftables, andjoinqueries. Forinstance,
SELECT id,name, price from PRODUCT
These queries arebuilt using specific keywords (ex:select, join), syntax and 
format using parameters (id,price ..)What isSQL?

Therelational databases support SQL. They aremade ofsetoftables.
each table contains asetoffields, andmany records.
Using sql,thedata must follow a strict schema (setoffields)
Each new record must have thesetoffields defined atthecreation ofthe 
table. Itcannot have more fields!Thedatabase structure: Relational

Atomicity : A transaction happens completely or not at all. Example : Bank Transfer
Consistency :Atransaction must keep thedatabase inavalid state .Example :Student
agemust begreater than 0
Isolation :Simultaneous transactions should notinterfere witheach other .
Durability :Once committed, data ispermanently saved .RDBMS: ACID

Nowadays, bigdata hasbecome tremendous due to:
Theexplosion ofsocial media sites such asFacebook andTwitter, user driven 
contents have increased rapidly. Hence, data, tobeanalysed andarchived, 
have increased involume andtend towards being semi structured and 
unstructured.
Monitoring systems likeGPS, sensors, automated trackers generate huge
volumes ofdata onaregular basis …Thechallenge ofbigdata
Thefactors above have made storing, analyzing, managing and
archiving these bigdata achallenging task!Horizontal scalability ?More volumes of data? 
Unstructured data, performance 
vs consistency

Vertical Scaling
Asto handle the larger volumes of data, vertical scaling isused in RDBMS( Relational 
Database Management System) .
It means increasing the capacity of a single database server by adding more hardware 
resources, including: CPU cores, More RAM, Faster storage, Better network capacity
Server     
4 CPU, 16GB
Server     
16 CPU, 64GBScaling up

Theremedy tothese 
challenges isNOSQL
10

Itstands forNotOnlySQL(NOSQL ).Itisanew setofdatabase that isnotbased on 
theRelational Database Management Systems principles.
NoSQL wasintroduced byCarl Strozzi on1998 toname hisfile-based database.
In2000 -Graph database Neo4j was launched.
2004 –Google BigTable waslaunched.
2005 -CouchDB islaunched.
2007 -Theresearch paper onAmazon Dynamo isreleased.
2008 -Facebooks open sources theCassandra project.
2009 -Theterm NoSQL was reintroduced.What isNOSQL?

NoSQL databases have thefollowing features incommon:
They donotuserelational data model
Donotfollow astrict schema
They runwell onclusters
Most ofthem are open source
They aremade forthenew generation ofweb applicationsWhat isNOSQL? (Con’t)

What isNOSQL? (structure)
Thestructure isasfollows: forexfbuses graphs based tostore firends with 
their friends …

Types ofNoSQL databases: Key-Value DB
Definition:
Value 1
Value 2•Thesimplest NoSQL database.
•Every single item intheDBisstored asanattribute name or“key”along withits
value .
•Data isstored asacollection ofkey/value pairs.
•Thekeyina key-value pairmust be unique .
•Thevalues canbeinteger, string orcomplex data types.
•Data isretrieved viaanexact match ofthekey.
Key 1
•New types ofdata canbeeasily added tothe
database asnew key-value pairs.
Key 2

Types ofNoSQL databases: Key-Value DataBase
Operations:
Here aretheoperations thatcanbeperformed on akey-value database are:
•putanew (key, value) :Insert thevalue associated withakey.
•get(key) :akeyispassed toretrieve thevalue associated withthekey.
•delete(key) :removes thevalue associated withakey.
•update (key, value) :Update thevalue associated withakey.

Types ofNoSQL databases: Key-Value DataBase
Operations:
put(key,value)

Types ofNoSQL databases: Key-Value DataBase
Advantages:
•Itisvery easy touse. Due tothesimplicity ofthedatabase, data canaccept any kind, oreven
different kinds when required.
•Itsresponse time isfastduetoitssimplicity, given thattheremaining environment near itis
very much constructed andimproved.
•Key-value store databases arescalable vertically aswellashorizontally.
1

Types ofNoSQL databases: Key-Value DataBase
Use cases:
•In-memory data caching :itisaneffective cache mechanism forfrequently accessed
butrarely updated data toaccelerate theresponse ofapplications =>faster read and
write .Hazelcast isanexample ofatechnology that provides anin-memory key-value
store forfastdata retrieval
•User preference storage and personalisation :itcanbeused toprovide recommendations
andstore personalized listsofitems forindividual clients. Forinstance e-commerce platforms,
they cancustomize/update adsaccordingly.

Types ofNoSQL databases?
Document Database (document -
oriented) :
•Document databases areconsidered tobenon-relational ornosql databases .
•Instead ofstoring data infixed rows and columns, document databases use flexible
documents .Foradocument database, youhave data store likeJSON object .Youdonot
require todefine which make itflexible .
•Document databases arethemost popular alternative totabular, relational databases .
Hence, what is adocument?

Types ofNoSQL databases?
Document Database (document -
oriented) :
•Adocument isarecord inadocument database .Adocument typically stores information
about oneobject andanyofitsrelated metadata .
•Documents store data infield-value pairs .The values can beavariety oftypes and
structures, including strings, numbers, dates, arrays, orobjects .Documents canbestored
informats likeJSON, andXML.

Types ofNoSQL databases?
Document Database (document -
oriented):
Here isaJSON document that
stores information about auser named Tom.

Document Database (document -
oriented):
Here isaJSON document that
stores information about auser named Tom.
But, what happens incase ofhaving many
Users?
Inthiscase, wetalkabout collections.
Sowhat iscollection?

Types ofNoSQL databases?
Document Database (document -oriented) :
•Acollection isagroup ofdocuments .Collections typically store documents that have
similar contents (similar toatable inRDBMS) .
•Notalldocuments inacollection arerequired tohave thesame fields ,because document
databases have aflexible schema .
•Note that some document databases provide schema validation, sotheschema can
optionally belocked down when needed .

NoSQL databases: Document based Database

NoSQL databases: Document based Database
Example :
○Forexample, following upthedocument fortheuser
Tom ,itcanbeadded toacollection named users .
○Also, ifconsidering anew user called Donna ,itcanbe
alsoadded tothecollection users .
○Hence, theusers Tom andDonna belong tothesame
collection .
○Note thatthedocument forDonna does notcontain
thesame fields asthedocument forTom.Theusers
collection isleveraging aflexible schema tostore the
information thatexists foreach user.

Why isiteasier towork with documents than tables
•Developers commonly findworking withdata indocuments tobeeasier andmore intuitive
than working withdata intables .
•Documents map todata structures inmost popular programming languages .Developers don't
have toworry about manually splitting related data across multiple tables when storing itor
joining itback together when retrieving it.
•JSON documents allow youtoembed related documents allowing easy fetching ofdata inone
single call.Therefore, storing data intheJSON format istruly anatural way tostore
information unlike theflatdata structure ofrows andcolumns .

NoSQL databases: Document based Database
CRUD operations :
•Create :Documents canbecreated inthedatabase .Each document hasaunique identifier .
•Read :Documents canberead from thedatabase .TheAPIorquery language allows developers
toquery fordocuments using their unique identifiers orfield values .Indexes canbeadded tothe
database inorder toincrease read performance .
•Update :Existing documents canbeupdated —either inwhole orinpart.
•Delete :Documents canbedeleted from thedatabase .

NoSQL databases: Document based Database
Use cases: both transactional andanalytical applications:
•Mobile applications
•Processing ofpayment operations
•Customer data management andpersonalization
•Internet of Things (IoT)
•Product catalogs andcontent management
•Operational analytics
•Real-time analytics

Examples ofdocument -based databases:
MongoDB, OrientDB, Apache CouchDB, IBMCloudant, CrateDB, BaseX, and many more

NoSQL databases: Graph based database
Definition:
•Data isorganized intheform ofagraph(directed graph with edges).
•Agraph dbcontains acollection ofnodes andedges.
•Anode represents anentity andanedge represents therelationship between two entities.
•Likemany different NoSQL databases these data models don’t have anyschema asit is
important because schema makes themodel wellandgood andeasy toedit.

NoSQL databases: Graph based database
Use cases :
•Social media analysis :Graph databases arecommonly used toanalyse social networks .A
heavy volume ofuser accounts(nodes) andmulti-dimensional connections .Agraph analysis for
asocial network candetermine :
oHow active areusers? (number ofnodes)
oWhich users have themost influence? (density ofconnections) …
•Credit card fraud :Asameans offraud detection, pattern identification isoften thefirstline
ofdefense thanks tographs based databases .

Types ofNoSQL DBs: Column based databases:
•Acolumn store database isatype ofdatabase thatstores data using acolumn oriented model .
•Columns store databases usesaconcept called keyspace .
•Akeyspace islikeaschema intherelational model. Thekeyspace contains allthecolumn
families (liketables incase ofrelational model).Definition:

Types ofNoSQL DBs: Column based databases:
•Columns store databases usesaconcept
called keyspace .
•Akeyspace islike aschema inthe
relational model .The keyspace contains all
thecolumn families (like tables incase of
relational model) .Definition:

Types ofNoSQL databases: Column based
Defintion :
•The column families aredefined initially, the
columns arenot.Columns canbeadded toany
column family atanytime.
•Unlike relational tables, column family’s schema is
notfixed ,and thistype ofdatabase does not
force individual rows tohave allthecolumns .
Example ofcolumn family UserProfile

Column -based nosql approach
Column based databases:
•Column family consists ofmultiple rows .
•Each rowcancontain adifferent number ofcolumns totheother rows .And thecolumns
don’t have tomatch thecolumns intheother rows (they canhave different column names,
data types) .
•Each column iscontained toitsrow.Itdoesn’t span allrows likeinarelational
database .Each column contains aname/value pair, along withatimestamp(date ofinsertion of
thedata) .

Column -based NoSql approach databases
•Row Key. Each rowhasaunique key, which isaunique identifier forthatrow.
•Column. Each column contains aname, avalue, andtimestamp.
•Name. This isthename ofthename/value pair.
•Value. This isthevalue ofthename/value pair.Row Structure:

Use cases:
•Thestorage oferror reports :The flexibility ofthese DBs allow theoptimal storage oferror 
reports generated byseveral applications, where each oneofthem canusethesuitable group’s 
column .
•Blogs data management :Blogs use these DBs tostore publications, their links, andthe
associated comments .
•Number ofvisitors reports :Thesystems forcounting thenumber ofvisitors usethese kinds of
databases thanks tothedynamic nature oftheir columns. This istoallow theclassification per 
page/site.
Massive datasets (billions of rows ), Time -series data, IoTsensor data, Event logging, 
Real -time analytics
